q: Heat is the form of energy that can be transferred from one system to another as a result of ___ difference.
Heat transfer and thermodynamics are two ___ branches of science.
Thermodynamics deals with ___ states by outlining the relationship between energy and work.
One of the basic requirement for heat transfer is the presence of a temperature ___.
There can be no net heat transfer between two systems that are at the ___ temperature.
In fluids, heat is mainly transferred by ___, in which the motion of the fluid itself carries heat from one place to another.
In solid, heat is transferred by ___ process in which heat energy is transferred among the adjacent parts of the material without the motion of the material itself.
The third way to transfer heat energy is by radiation process in the form of ___ waves.
The individual parts of a medium do not move as a ___ in heat conduction.
Heat energy is transferred by successive ___ among the adjacent parts in heat conduction.
When heat is transferred via conduction, heat energy is transferred internally by vibrations of ___ and molecules.
Metals have many free ___. Since these free electrons can carry heat, metals are generally very good conductors of heat.
The spoon which acts as a medium for heat transfer, does not ___ at all.
The rate of heat conduction through a material of cross-sectional area $A$ is proportional to the temperature difference $\Delta T$, but is inversely proportional to the ___ $l$ of the material.
The variable $k$ is the ___ conductivity of the material, which is a measure of the ability of a material to conduct heat.
In SI units, $k$ is watt per metre per ___ ($\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-1}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$).
The ratio $\frac{T_2 - T_1}{l}$ is called temperature ___.
Temperature gradient is also expressed in differential form as ___.
In one dimensional form, $\frac{H}{A} = -k \frac{\text{d}T}{\text{d}x}$ where $\frac{H}{A}$ is heat flow per unit area and $H$ is heat ___.
This mathematical model of heat conduction was first formulated by the French physicist Jean Baptiste Joseph ___ in his Fourier's law of heat conduction.
Fourier's law states that the rate of heat flow per unit area (heat flux density) is proportional to the magnitude of temperature ___.
The ___ sign indicates that temperature $T$ must decrease as distance $x$ increases.
In the SI units, heat flux density is watt per metre squared ($\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}$) and heat current is ___ ($\text{W}$).
The thermal conductivity of a material can be defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature ___.
A high value for thermal conductivity indicates that the material is a good thermal ___.
A low value indicates that the material is a poor thermal conductor or thermal ___.
Materials such as copper and silver that are good electric conductors are also good ___ conductors.
Materials such as rubber, wood, and styrofoam are poor conductors of heat and have ___ conductivity values.
Actually, the major heat transfer in human beings is due to the flow of warm ___.
Heat convection is the flow of heat through a fluid from places of higher temperature to places of lower temperature by ___ of the fluid itself.
In heat convection, the transfer of energy occurs between an object and its environment due to fluid ___.
Convection currents are set up in the fluid because the hotter part of the fluid is not as ___ as the cooler part.
There is an upward ___ force on the hotter fluid, making it rise while the cooler and denser fluid sinks.
Although some heat may be transferred by conduction in liquids and gases, a much larger quantity of heat is carried by the ___ of the fluid itself.
Convection occurs only in a liquid or a gas, never in a ___.
___ force is what drives natural convection.
The basic equation for the rate of convection heat transfer is known as Newton's law of ___.
In the equation $H_{\text{conv}} = q A (T_2 - T_1)$, $q$ is the convective heat transfer ___ (heat convection constant).
In SI units, $q$ is expressed as watt per metre squared per ___ ($\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$).
When $T_2 > T_1$, heat is gained by the object and $H_{\text{conv}}$ is ___.
When $T_2 < T_1$, heat is lost by the object and $H_{\text{conv}}$ is ___.
The heat loss by ___ is important for living beings.
The household hot water system is also designed based on the process of heat convection in ___.
Hot water from the boiler rises and flows into the tank where it rises to the ___.
When you use a hot water tap at home, you draw hot water from the top of the ___.

Since convective heat transfer coefficient of air is very much less than $5000\,\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$, the chip cannot be cooled with air. Therefore it will require the ___ convection.
Heat radiation is a transfer of heat that does not require a material ___.
Heat is transferred by thermal radiation which are ___ waves that pass through a medium or even vacuum.
Unlike conduction and convection, the transfer of energy by radiation does not require an ___ medium.
Energy transfer by radiation is the fastest (at the speed of ___) and it suffers no attenuation in a vacuum.
This is how the energy of the sun reaches the earth by heat ___.
Thermal radiation is the form of radiation emitted by bodies because of their ___.
All bodies at a temperature above absolute ___ emit thermal radiation.
All solids, liquids and gases emit or ___ radiation to varying degrees.
Radiation is mostly considered to be a ___ phenomenon for solids that are opaque to thermal radiation.
The radiation incident on opaque solids is usually absorbed within a few ___ from the surface.
If an object has a good rate of emission of radiation, then it also has a good rate of ___.
The radiation emitted by a blackbody is called blackbody ___.
The total ___ power is defined as the total radiant energy of different wavelengths emitted from unit area of a surface of a body in one second.
The total emissive power of a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of ___ temperature.
Thermodynamics is the study of systems involving energy in the form of ___ and work.
A good example of a thermodynamic system is gas confined by a piston in a ___.
When two systems are in thermal equilibrium, there is no net ___ between them.
The zeroth law of thermodynamics is a simple definition of ___.
The first law of thermodynamics relates the change in ___ of a system to heat added and the work done by the system.
The change in internal energy $\Delta U$ of a system is equal to the net heat flow $Q$ into the system minus the total ___ done by the system.
$Q$ is positive if heat is added to the system, and ___ if heat is removed.
The internal energy $U$ of a system is defined as the sum of the energies of all the ___ particles.
The internal energy of ideal gases depends only on ___, not on volume or pressure.
Heat transfer and work are both energy in ___.
Internal energy is a form of energy completely different from either heat or ___.
A thermodynamic process may be defined as a change from one ___ state of a system to another.
In thermodynamics, a quasi-static process is a thermodynamic process that happens slowly enough for the system to remain in internal physical ___.
An isobaric process occurs at constant ___.
The work done by the system in an isobaric process is simply the pressure multiplied by the change in ___.
An isochoric process occurs at constant ___.
When the volume of a thermodynamic system is constant, no work is done by the system because ___ as shown in Figure 5.8.
According to the first law, the change in internal energy must equal any heat flowing into or out of the system. That is, ___.
The change in internal energy is expressed in terms of molar heat capacity ___.
An isothermal process occurs at a constant ___.
However, for an ideal gas, internal energy $U$ is only a function of the ___.
Since $\Delta U = 0$, according to the first law, ___.
If the system does the work, the energy comes from heat flowing into the system from the ___.
In an adiabatic process, there is no heat transfer into or out of the system, i.e. ___.
This process can be done either by enclosing the system in a thermal insulator or by carrying out the process so ___ that there is no enough time for heat transfer.
The first law of thermodynamics $\Delta U = Q - W$ is thus reduced to ___.
where $\gamma$ is the ratio of the ___.
Heat engine is a system that converts heat to ___, which can then be used to do mechanical work.
A series of processes in which a system is brought back to its initial state is called a ___.
The thermal efficiency of a heat engine is the ratio of ___ to heat input.
Applying the first law of thermodynamic to one complete cycle, remembering that there is no net change of internal energy, we get, ___.
There are two types of internal combustion engine (gasoline engine and diesel engine) that generates the input heat by the combustion of fuel ___ the engine itself.
In the gasoline engines, the combustion of the gasoline and air takes place explosively by an ___.
The cycle involves the performance of six processes, of which four require motion of the piston and are called ___.
A mixture of gasoline vapour and air is drawn into the cylinder by the ___ stroke of the piston.
The mixture of gasoline vapour and air is compressed until its pressure and temperature rise ___.
Combustion of the hot mixture is caused to take place very rapidly by an electric spark. The volume remains ___ during this process.
The hot combustion products expand and push the piston out, thus suffering a drop in ___ and temperature.
An exhaust valve allows some gas to escape until the pressure drops to that of the ___.
The piston pushes almost all the remaining combustion products out of the cylinder by exerting a pressure sufficiently larger than that of the outside ___.
Theoretical efficiency of a gasoline engine is $\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{(V_1/V_2)^{\gamma-1}}$, where $V_1/V_2$ = ___.
For a typical compression ratio of 8 and with $\gamma = 1.4$, $\eta$ = ___ for an ideal gasoline engine.
Unlike the spark-ignition gasoline engine, the diesel engine induces only ___ into the combustion chamber on its intake stroke.
Diesel fuel is sprayed into the cylinder by the ___ and immediately ignites.
The ___ created by the combustion of the fuel drives the piston downward.
As the piston nears the bottom of its stroke, all of the ___ open.
Exhaust gases rush out of the cylinder, relieving the ___.
As the piston is at the bottom, it uncovers the ___ ports.
Pressurized air fills the cylinder, forcing out the remainder of the ___.
The exhaust valves close and the piston starts traveling back upward, re-covering the intake ports and compressing the fresh air. This is the ___ stroke.
Carnot engine sets an upper limit on the efficiency of all other real engines, such as steam engines, gasoline and ___ engines.
Every point along the path of a reversible process is in an ___ state.
The working substance of a Carnot engine is an ___ gas contained in a cylinder fitted with a movable piston at one end.
In step 1 of the Carnot cycle, gas expansion is propelled by absorption of heat from the ___ temperature reservoir.
Thermal efficiency of a heat engine $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of the work output $W$ to the heat input ___.
Carnot's theorem states that no engine operating between two heat reservoirs can be more efficient than a ___ engine operating between the same reservoirs.
The rate of heat flow $H$ is the amount of heat energy transferred per unit ___.
The object which can absorb all the electromagnetic radiations falling upon it is called a ___.
Heat engine is a system that converts heat to ___ energy, which can then be used to perform mechanical work.
Heat conduction is one mode of energy transfer where individual parts of a medium do not move as a whole in ___.
In an irreversible process, the system cannot be returned to its initial condition along the same path on the ___ diagram.
The most important condition for a process to be reversible is that the process should be carried out infinitesimally ___.
The Carnot cycle consists of two ___ processes and two adiabatic processes.
During step 2 of the Carnot cycle, the piston and cylinder are assumed to be thermally ___.
In step 3 of the Carnot cycle, the gas is in thermal contact with the ___ reservoir.
The efficiency $\eta$ is given by $1 - ___$.
Kelvin proposed that we use a Carnot engine as a ___.
Temperatures of the reservoirs in the efficiency formula must be ___ temperature.
The temperature of the diesel burning is ___ K.
Heat convection is the flow of heat through a ___ by movement of the fluid itself.
The process where individual parts of a medium do not move as a whole is called ___.
The Carnot cycle was described by a French ___ named Sadi Carnot.
During step 4 of the Carnot cycle, the ___ do work on the gas.
Real engines are less efficient due to friction and energy losses by ___.
The ratio of heat absorbed by the engine to the heat rejected defines the ratio of two ___.
A corollary states that all reversible engines operating between the same heat reservoirs are equally ___.
The gas expansion in step 2 causes it to cool to the ___ temperature.
Heat radiation passes through a medium or even ___.
A Carnot engine operates on an ideal ___ cycle.
The temperature of the steam engine is 373 K and outside room temperature is ___ K.
